message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "How does AGE affect the ability to comprehend ACCELERATED / TIME-COMPRESSED / fast speech (i.e. "speed listening")? Cover the full human lifespan across these angles: (1) DEVELOPMENTAL curve in childhood and adolescence — at what age does the ability to understand time-compressed/fast speech mature, and how do children compare to young adults? (2) AGING decline — how does comprehension of time-compressed speech change in older adults (50s through 80s), how steep is the decline, and at what age does it begin? Give quantitative thresholds (syllables/second, words/minute, or % time-compression) by age band wherever available. (3) MECHANISMS — disentangle peripheral hearing loss (presbycusis) from central/cognitive factors: central auditory temporal processing, cognitive/perceptual processing speed, and working-memory / phonological-loop capacity; which factor dominates the age effect, and does the age penalty persist after controlling for audibility? (4) PEAK age — at what age is accelerated-speech comprehension at its maximum, and how wide is the plateau? (5) TRAINABILITY / PLASTICITY across age — can older adults still train to improve speed-listening comprehension and by how much, and how does AGE OF ONSET of blindness (early-blind vs late-blind) modulate the ultra-fast-speech advantage and its neural (visual-cortex) plasticity? CONTEXT: this extends prior research on speed-listening; relate findings to a healthy adult who currently comprehends roughly 13 syllables/second of time-compressed audiobook narration. Prefer peer-reviewed sources; provide quantitative figures and effect sizes by age group where possible, and flag where peripheral hearing loss is or isn't controlled for."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-05882-5
**Title:** Speech recognition in noise across the life span with cognition and hearing sensitivity as mediators of age effects (Scientific Reports, 2025)
**Found via:** Cognitive processing speed & working-memory/phonological-loop mediators search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
# Article: "Speech Recognition in Noise Across the Life Span with Cognition and Hearing Sensitivity as Mediators of Age Effects"

**Publication Date:** 2025 (July 1, 2025)

---

## (1) Age Range and Trajectory by Age Band

**Sample:** 357 participants aged 7–90 years (M = 27.2, SD = 20.8)

**Five developmental groups analyzed:**
- Middle childhood (7–12 years): SRTn = −5.74 dB SNR
- Adolescence (13–20 years): SRTn = −7.16 dB SNR
- Early adulthood (21–40 years): SRTn = −8.19 dB SNR [BEST PERFORMANCE]
- Middle adulthood (41–65 years): SRTn = −6.49 dB SNR
- Late adulthood (66+ years): SRTn = −4.93 dB SNR [WORST PERFORMANCE]

Quote: "optimal speech perception performance occurred during early adulthood (21–40 years), with performance increasing at the fastest rate from 7 to 20 years old"

---

## (2) Quantitative Findings – Variance Explained & Effect Sizes

**SEM Model Results:**
- Quote: "the predictors in the model explained 61% of the individual variation in SRTn"
- Polynomial model (continuous age): R² = 0.426 for SRTn trajectory
- **Peak performance age:** 37.8 years (SRTn minimum)

**Cognitive decline onset:**
- Auditory working memory (DBS): peaks ~38.1 years
- Processing speed (TMT-A): turns at 36.8 years
- Processing speed (TMT-B): turns at 31.9 years

**Hearing sensitivity (PTA4):**
- Quote: "hearing sensitivity (PTA4) begins to deteriorate around age 17"
- Crosses clinical threshold (20 dB HL) at ~68.7 years

---

## (3) Age Penalty After Controlling for Hearing Sensitivity

**Affirmative – age effect persists independently:**

Quote: "cognitive abilities have a dominant effect on performance in auditory challenging conditions compared to hearing sensitivity" especially in younger adults with normal audiograms.

The SEM revealed: "a direct negative linear (β = −0.42, p < .001) and a direct positive squared effect (β = 0.29, p < .001)" of age on SRTn beyond mediation pathways.

---

## (4) Peripheral Hearing vs. Central/Cognitive Dominance

**Cognition dominates the age effect:**

Quote: "processing speed and auditory working memory emerged as significant mediators...with all effects reaching statistical significance" while hearing sensitivity showed only "modest" contribution (β = 0.09, p = .042).

Key quote: "cognitive efficiency is central to maintaining auditory performance under challenging conditions, especially in the context of age-related changes."

The model showed processing speed indirect effect (β = −0.25 to 0.40) and working memory (β = −0.13 to 0.16) substantially exceeded hearing's contribution.

---

## (5) Peak Performance Age

**Peak age: Late 30s (~37.8 years)**

Quote: "The turning point of this curve was estimated at 37.8 years, marking the age at which speech perception in noise begins to decline."

This coincides precisely with cognitive inflection points (working memory ~38 years; processing speed 32–37 years), but **15+ years before clinical hearing loss emerges** (~68.7 years).

---

## (6) Publication Date

**2025, July 1** — *Scientific Reports*, Vol. 15, Article 20575

content.text: I have the full content. Note the article studies speech-in-noise (not time-compressed speech specifically), so claims are supporting/tangential to the speed-listening question, but directly relevant to mechanisms (cognition vs hearing mediating age effects) and the peak-age / lifespan-trajectory angles. Let me verify a couple of the specific quotes by fetching the methods/results sections to ensure verbatim accuracy.

input.prompt: Return the EXACT verbatim sentences from this article (quote precisely, word for word) that state: (a) the total number of participants and their age range; (b) the statement about optimal/peak speech perception performance occurring during early adulthood and the fastest rate of improvement; (c) the estimated turning-point age of the SRTn curve (around 37-38 years); (d) the statement that cognitive abilities / cognition have a dominant effect compared to hearing sensitivity; (e) the percentage of variance in SRTn explained by the model; (f) the statement about when hearing sensitivity begins to deteriorate. Give the sentences exactly as written.

content.content:
# Exact Verbatim Quotes from Article

**(a) Total participants and age range:**
"A total of 357 participants, ranging in age from 7 to 90 years (Mage = 27.2, SD = 20.8), were included in the study."

**(b) Optimal performance in early adulthood with fastest improvement rate:**
"optimal speech perception performance occurred during early adulthood (21–40 years), with performance increasing at the fastest rate from 7 to 20 years old"

**(c) Estimated turning-point age of SRTn curve:**
"The turning point of this curve was estimated at 37.8 years, marking the age at which speech perception in noise begins to decline."

**(d) Cognition has dominant effect compared to hearing sensitivity:**
"This measurable early change in hearing sensitivity did not significantly impact speech perception in noise until the onset of an evident decline in cognitive abilities, emphasizing the importance of the latter to speech perception in noise"

**(e) Percentage of variance explained by model:**
"Altogether, the predictors in the model explained 61% of the individual variation in SRTn."

**(f) When hearing sensitivity begins to deteriorate:**
"The fitted curve demonstrated a gradual increase in hearing thresholds across the lifespan, with a turning point estimated at approximately 17 years of age."

content.text: I now have verified verbatim quotes. This is a primary peer-reviewed research article in Scientific Reports (Nature Portfolio). The study is on speech-in-noise rather than time-compressed speech, so it's supporting/tangential for the speed-listening question, but directly addresses the lifespan trajectory, peak age, and the cognition-vs-hearing mechanism dissociation. Let me compile the structured output.

claims.claim: Speech-in-noise recognition (SRTn) follows an inverted-U lifespan trajectory peaking in early adulthood: it improves fastest from ages 7-20, reaches optimal performance during 21-40 years, then declines, with the curve's turning point estimated at 37.8 years (n=357, ages 7-90).
claims.quote: optimal speech perception performance occurred during early adulthood (21–40 years), with performance increasing at the fastest rate from 7 to 20 years old ... The turning point of this curve was estimated at 37.8 years, marking the age at which speech perception in noise begins to decline.

claims.claim: Cognition (processing speed and auditory working memory) dominates the age effect on speech-in-noise recognition over peripheral hearing sensitivity; early-onset hearing-threshold change does not meaningfully impair performance until cognitive decline begins, so the age penalty is driven by central/cognitive rather than peripheral factors.
claims.quote: This measurable early change in hearing sensitivity did not significantly impact speech perception in noise until the onset of an evident decline in cognitive abilities, emphasizing the importance of the latter to speech perception in noise

claims.claim: Hearing sensitivity (PTA4) begins to deteriorate in adolescence (turning point ~17 years), more than a decade before speech-in-noise performance peaks (~38 years), demonstrating that measurable audibility decline does not by itself drive the age-related comprehension decline.
claims.quote: The fitted curve demonstrated a gradual increase in hearing thresholds across the lifespan, with a turning point estimated at approximately 17 years of age.

claims.claim: A structural-equation model with age, cognition, and hearing as predictors (mediation framework) explained 61% of the individual variance in speech-reception thresholds in noise.
claims.quote: Altogether, the predictors in the model explained 61% of the individual variation in SRTn.

claims.claim: The peak-performance age for speech-in-noise (~37.8 years) coincides with the inflection points of auditory working memory (~38 years) and processing speed (~32-37 years), but precedes the clinical-hearing-loss threshold crossing (~68.7 years) by roughly 30 years, indicating cognitive rather than peripheral timing of the age effect.
claims.quote: The turning point of this curve was estimated at 37.8 years, marking the age at which speech perception in noise begins to decline.

content.text:
Extraction complete. The Nature.com URL redirected to an authentication wall, so I retrieved the full open-access text via PubMed Central (PMC12215552, https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12215552/) and verified all quotes verbatim from the source.

Summary: This is a primary peer-reviewed study (Scientific Reports, July 1, 2025, n=357, ages 7-90). It is on speech-in-noise, not time-compressed/accelerated speech specifically, so its claims are supporting/tangential to the speed-listening question rather than central. Its directly relevant contributions to the research question are:

- Mechanisms (angle 3, the strongest fit): cognition (processing speed + auditory working memory) mediates the age effect more than peripheral hearing; the age penalty persists and is driven by central/cognitive factors, with measurable hearing-threshold decline not impairing performance until cognitive decline sets in.
- Peak age / plateau (angle 4): inverted-U with peak ~37.8 years; fastest developmental gains from ages 7-20.
- Aging onset (angle 2): decline begins around late 30s, decades before clinical hearing loss (~68.7 years).

Caveat for the parent agent: hearing sensitivity (PTA4 audibility) IS measured and modeled here, so audibility is controlled for — but the outcome is speech-in-noise SRT, not time-compressed-speech comprehension, so transfer to the "13 syllables/second time-compressed audiobook" context is by analogy only.
